% title: AcyclicityUnderIntervention
\def\rowref{claim\_3\_13}

%
% Cross-references: see claim_<ref>_statement_<title>.tex in this folder
% for the corresponding statement.

% --- statement (restated) ---------------------------------------------------
\begin{Rem}[AcyclicityUnderIntervention]
    If a CDMG $G=(J,V,E,L)$ is acyclic then also $G_{\doit(I_W)}$ is acyclic and a topological order for
    $G_{\doit(I_W)}$ is also one for $G$.
    Any topological order of $G$ can be extended to one for $G_{\doit(I_W)}$, e.g.\
    by putting all the $I_w$ nodes first in the ordering.
\end{Rem}

% --- proof ------------------------------------------------------------------
% Proof constructed from scratch in the LN paradigm: the LN's \Rem at
% lecture-notes/lecture_notes/graphs.tex:821--829 contains no \begin{proof}
% block (the next claimmark, claim_3_14, follows immediately at line 831).
% The three parts mirror the three theorems in the companion Lean file
% AcyclicityUnderInterventionNodes.lean (parts A / B / C correspond to
% isAcyclic_..., ..._restrict, ..._extend respectively); the named order in
% Part C mirrors \refrow{claim_3_6}'s standalone splitOrder relation.
\begin{proof}
The whole argument rests on a single edge-set observation: by \refrow{def_3_13} item iii,
\[
  E_{\doit(I_W)}
  \;=\;
  E \;\dcup\; \lC I_w \tuh w \,\middle|\, w \in W \sm J \rC,
\]
i.e.\ every edge of $G_{\doit(I_W)}$ is either an original edge of $G$ or a fresh edge $I_w \tuh w$ for some $w \in W \sm J$. In particular, the \refrow{def_3_1} invariant $E \ins (J \cup V) \times V$ together with the fresh-edge target $w \in W \sm J \ins V$ (using the precondition $W \ins J \cup V$ to rule out $w \in W \sm J$ being outside $G$, leaving $w \in V$) gives that \emph{every} edge of $G_{\doit(I_W)}$ has its target in $V$:
\[
  E_{\doit(I_W)} \;\ins\; \lp J_{\doit(I_W)} \cup V_{\doit(I_W)} \rp \times V.
\]
By contrast, the \emph{source} of an edge can be either an original node $v' \in J \cup V$ (first disjunct, $E$-piece) or a fresh intervention node $I_w$ (second disjunct). This source--target asymmetry is the key observation driving all three parts below.

\medskip\noindent\emph{Part A (acyclicity).}
Suppose, for contradiction, that $G_{\doit(I_W)}$ is not acyclic. By \refrow{def_3_6} there is a node $v$ of $G_{\doit(I_W)}$ admitting a non-trivial directed walk to itself:
\[
  v = v_0 \tuh v_1 \tuh \cdots \tuh v_n = v
  \qquad \text{in } G_{\doit(I_W)}, \quad n \ge 1,
\]
i.e.\ $(v_k, v_{k+1}) \in E_{\doit(I_W)}$ for every $k = 0, \dots, n-1$ (\refrow{def_3_4}).

In a directed cycle every vertex is the target of some edge: for each $k \in \lC 1, \dots, n \rC$ the step $(v_{k-1}, v_k) \in E_{\doit(I_W)}$ has $v_k$ as its target, and the closure $v_0 = v_n$ then makes $v_0 = v_n$ a target as well. By the edge-set observation above, every target of an edge of $G_{\doit(I_W)}$ lies in $V$. Hence $v_k \in V$ for all $k = 0, \dots, n$; in particular no $v_k$ is one of the fresh $I_w$ intervention nodes.

We now show each step is an original $E$-edge. Fix $k$ and consider $(v_{k-1}, v_k) \in E_{\doit(I_W)}$. By \refrow{def_3_13} item iii it is either an original edge $(v_{k-1}, v_k) \in E$, or a fresh edge $I_w \tuh w$ for some $w \in W \sm J$. The fresh-edge case is impossible: its source is $I_w$, a fresh intervention node, but we just showed $v_{k-1} \in V$ is original. So $(v_{k-1}, v_k) \in E$.

The same vertex sequence
\[
  v = v_0 \tuh v_1 \tuh \cdots \tuh v_n = v
  \qquad \text{in } G
\]
is therefore a non-trivial directed walk in $G$ (\refrow{def_3_4}). Since $v_0 \in V$, in particular $v_0$ is a node of $G$ (\refrow{def_3_2}), so $v_0$ admits a non-trivial directed walk to itself in $G$, contradicting acyclicity of $G$ (\refrow{def_3_6}).

\medskip\noindent\emph{Part B (restriction direction).}
Assume $W \ins J \cup V$ and that $<'$ is a topological order of $G_{\doit(I_W)}$ (\refrow{def_3_8}). We claim that the restriction
\[
  v < w \;:\iff\; v <' w \qquad (v, w \in J \cup V)
\]
is a topological order of $G$. Note that the canonical embedding $J \cup V \hookrightarrow J_{\doit(I_W)} \cup V_{\doit(I_W)}$ is the identity: by \refrow{def_3_13} items i.--ii., $J_{\doit(I_W)} = J \dcup \lC I_w \mid w \in W \sm J \rC$ and $V_{\doit(I_W)} = V$, so $J \ins J_{\doit(I_W)}$ and $V = V_{\doit(I_W)}$, giving $J \cup V \ins J_{\doit(I_W)} \cup V_{\doit(I_W)}$. Hence every $v \in G$ is also a node of $G_{\doit(I_W)}$ (\refrow{def_3_2}). We verify the four conditions of \refrow{def_3_8} in turn.

\smallskip
\noindent\emph{Irreflexivity on $G$.} For $v \in G = J \cup V$, the embedding gives $v \in G_{\doit(I_W)}$, so $\neg(v <' v)$ by irreflexivity of $<'$. By definition of $<$, this is $\neg(v < v)$. \checkmark

\smallskip
\noindent\emph{Transitivity on $G$.} For $v, w, x \in G$, all three lie in $G_{\doit(I_W)}$ by the embedding. If $v < w$ and $w < x$, unfolding the definition gives $v <' w$ and $w <' x$, hence $v <' x$ by transitivity of $<'$, i.e.\ $v < x$. \checkmark

\smallskip
\noindent\emph{Trichotomy on $G$.} For $v, w \in G$, the embedding gives $v, w \in G_{\doit(I_W)}$, and trichotomy of $<'$ yields $v <' w \lor v = w \lor w <' v$, equivalently $v < w \lor v = w \lor w < v$. \checkmark

\smallskip
\noindent\emph{Parents precede children.} Suppose $v \in \Pa^G(w)$. By \refrow{def_3_5} this means $v \in G$ together with $(v, w) \in E$. The first disjunct of \refrow{def_3_13} item iii embeds $E$ into $E_{\doit(I_W)}$, so $(v, w) \in E_{\doit(I_W)}$. Combined with $v \in G_{\doit(I_W)}$ (from the embedding), \refrow{def_3_5} gives $v \in \Pa^{G_{\doit(I_W)}}(w)$. The topological-order condition for $<'$ on $G_{\doit(I_W)}$ then yields $v <' w$, i.e.\ $v < w$. \checkmark

Combining the four checks, $<$ is a topological order of $G$ in the sense of \refrow{def_3_8}.

\medskip\noindent\emph{Part C (extension direction with explicit recipe).}
Conversely, assume $W \ins J \cup V$ and that $<$ is a topological order of $G$. We construct a topological order $<'$ of $G_{\doit(I_W)}$ by the LN's recipe: put all $I_w$ nodes first, ordered among themselves by the underlying $w \in W \sm J$ values, and inherit $<$ on the original nodes. Explicitly, on the carrier
\[
  J_{\doit(I_W)} \cup V_{\doit(I_W)}
  \;=\; (J \cup V) \,\dcup\, \lC I_w \,\middle|\, w \in W \sm J \rC
\]
(by \refrow{def_3_13} items i.--ii.\ and the precondition $W \ins J \cup V$), define
\[
  a <' b \;:\iff\;
  \begin{cases}
    a < b & \text{if } a, b \in J \cup V \text{ (both original),} \\
    w_1 < w_2 & \text{if } a = I_{w_1}, \ b = I_{w_2} \text{ (both fresh),} \\
    \mathrm{true} & \text{if } a = I_w \text{ (fresh), } b \in J \cup V \text{ (original),} \\
    \mathrm{false} & \text{if } a \in J \cup V \text{ (original), } b = I_w \text{ (fresh).}
  \end{cases}
\]
The four cases are mutually exclusive and exhaustive because $J \cup V$ and $\lC I_w \mid w \in W \sm J \rC$ are disjoint (the second component of the disjoint union in $J_{\doit(I_W)}$, \refrow{def_3_13} item i.). We verify the four conditions of \refrow{def_3_8} by case analysis on which quadrant each vertex lies in.

\smallskip
\noindent\emph{Irreflexivity.} Let $a \in J_{\doit(I_W)} \cup V_{\doit(I_W)}$.
\begin{itemize}
  \item $a \in J \cup V$ (original): $a <' a$ unfolds to $a < a$, false by irreflexivity of $<$ on $G$.
  \item $a = I_w$ (fresh): $a <' a$ unfolds to $w < w$, false by irreflexivity of $<$ on $G$ (using $w \in W \sm J \ins V \ins G$ from the precondition $W \ins J \cup V$).
\end{itemize}
In neither case does $a <' a$ hold. \checkmark

\smallskip
\noindent\emph{Transitivity.} Let $a, b, c \in J_{\doit(I_W)} \cup V_{\doit(I_W)}$ with $a <' b$ and $b <' c$; we show $a <' c$. We label each vertex by its quadrant -- $O$ for original ($\in J \cup V$) or $F$ for fresh ($= I_w$ for some $w \in W \sm J$) -- and case-split on the triple $(a, b, c) \in \lC O, F \rC^3$. The key constraint is that any cross step $O \to F$ has truth value $\mathrm{false}$ (by definition of $<'$), so any case in which either premise $a <' b$ or $b <' c$ is of the form $O \to F$ is vacuous. The eight cases split as follows.
\begin{itemize}
  \item $OOO$: $a <' b$ and $b <' c$ unfold to $a < b$ and $b < c$ in $G$; transitivity of $<$ gives $a < c$, i.e.\ $a <' c$. \checkmark
  \item $FFF$: write $a = I_u, b = I_v, c = I_w$ with $u, v, w \in W \sm J \ins G$ (using the precondition $W \ins J \cup V$). The premises unfold to $u < v$ and $v < w$ in $G$; transitivity of $<$ gives $u < w$, i.e.\ $a <' c$. \checkmark
  \item $FFO$ ($a = I_u, b = I_v, c \in J \cup V$): the first premise unfolds to $u < v$; the second premise is the cross slot $F \to O = \mathrm{true}$. The conclusion $a <' c$ is again the cross slot $F \to O = \mathrm{true}$. \checkmark
  \item $FOO$ ($a = I_u, b, c \in J \cup V$): the first premise is the cross slot $F \to O = \mathrm{true}$; the second premise unfolds to $b < c$. The conclusion $a <' c$ is again the cross slot $F \to O = \mathrm{true}$. \checkmark
  \item $OOF, OFO, OFF, FOF$: each of these has at least one premise of the form $O \to F$, which is $\mathrm{false}$. The hypothesis is vacuous and the implication holds trivially. \checkmark
\end{itemize}
In every non-vacuous case the conclusion $a <' c$ holds. \checkmark

\smallskip
\noindent\emph{Trichotomy.} Let $a, b \in J_{\doit(I_W)} \cup V_{\doit(I_W)}$.
\begin{itemize}
  \item Both original ($a, b \in J \cup V$): trichotomy of $<$ on $G$ gives $a < b \lor a = b \lor b < a$, which unfolds to $a <' b \lor a = b \lor b <' a$.
  \item Both fresh ($a = I_{w_1}, b = I_{w_2}$): trichotomy of $<$ on $G$ at $w_1, w_2 \in W \sm J \ins G$ gives $w_1 < w_2 \lor w_1 = w_2 \lor w_2 < w_1$. The middle disjunct $w_1 = w_2$ gives $I_{w_1} = I_{w_2}$, and the other two unfold to $a <' b$ and $b <' a$.
  \item Cross ($a = I_w$ fresh, $b \in J \cup V$ original): the cross slot $a <' b$ is $\mathrm{true}$, so the first disjunct of trichotomy holds.
  \item Cross ($a \in J \cup V$ original, $b = I_w$ fresh): symmetrically, $b <' a$ is $\mathrm{true}$, so the third disjunct holds.
\end{itemize}
In every case at least one of $a <' b$, $a = b$, $b <' a$ holds. \checkmark

\smallskip
\noindent\emph{Parents precede children.} Suppose $a \in \Pa^{G_{\doit(I_W)}}(b)$, i.e.\ $a \in G_{\doit(I_W)}$ and $(a, b) \in E_{\doit(I_W)}$ (\refrow{def_3_5}). By \refrow{def_3_13} item iii, either $(a, b) \in E$ (an original edge) or $(a, b) = (I_w, w)$ for some $w \in W \sm J$ (a fresh edge).
\begin{itemize}
  \item Original edge $(a, b) \in E$: both endpoints lie in $J \cup V$ (\refrow{def_3_1}), and the source observation $a \in G$ (\refrow{def_3_5}) together with the edge $(a, b) \in E$ yields $a \in \Pa^G(b)$. The topological-order condition for $<$ on $G$ gives $a < b$, which by definition is $a <' b$.
  \item Fresh edge $(a, b) = (I_w, w)$: the source is fresh and the target $w \in W \sm J \ins V \ins J \cup V$ is original. So $a <' b$ unfolds to the cross slot $\mathrm{true}$ and holds directly.
\end{itemize}
In both cases $a <' b$. \checkmark

Combining the four checks, $<'$ is a topological order of $G_{\doit(I_W)}$ in the sense of \refrow{def_3_8}, and by construction it extends $<$ in the sense that the restriction $a <' b \iff a < b$ holds for all $a, b \in J \cup V$ (first quadrant of the case split).
\end{proof}

\end{document}

  • Cross ($a \in J \cup V$ original, $b = I_w$ fresh): symmetrically, $b <' a$ is $\mathrm{true}$, so the third disjunct holds.

Parents precede children. Suppose $a \in \Pa^{G_{\doit(I_W)}}(b)$, i.e.\ $a \in G_{\doit(I_W)}$ and $(a, b) \in E_{\doit(I_W)}$ (def_3_5). By def_3_13 item iii, either $(a, b) \in E$ (an original edge) or $(a, b) = (I_w, w)$ for some $w \in W \sm J$ (a fresh edge).

  • Original edge $(a, b) \in E$: both endpoints lie in $J \cup V$ (def_3_1), and the source observation $a \in G$ (def_3_5) together with the edge $(a, b) \in E$ yields $a \in \Pa^G(b)$. The topological-order condition for $<$ on $G$ gives $a < b$, which by definition is $a <' b$.
  • Fresh edge $(a, b) = (I_w, w)$: the source is fresh and the target $w \in W \sm J \ins V \ins J \cup V$ is original. So $a <' b$ unfolds to the cross slot $\mathrm{true}$ and holds directly.

In both cases $a <' b$. \checkmark

Combining the four checks, $<'$ is a topological order of $G_{\doit(I_W)}$ in the sense of def_3_8, and by construction it extends $<$ in the sense that the restriction $a <' b \iff a < b$ holds for all $a, b \in J \cup V$ (first quadrant of the case split).

/-- claim_3_13 part A: if `G` is acyclic and `W ⊆ G.J ∪ G.V`, then
`G.extendingCDMGWithInterventionNodes W hW` is acyclic. Mirrors the
acyclicity half of the `\Rem` immediately after def_3_13 in
`lecture-notes/lecture_notes/graphs.tex` (lines 821 -- 829).

The mathematical content is that every edge of the extended graph
has target `Sum.inl _` (`mem_extendingCDMGWithInterventionNodes_E`:
either it is a `Sum.inl × Sum.inl`-lift of some `(v', w') ∈ G.E`, or
it is a fresh `Sum.inr ⟨w, _⟩ → Sum.inl w`, so the target is
`Sum.inl _` in both cases), so a directed cycle cannot pass through
a `Sum.inr` vertex -- there is no edge into a `Sum.inr` vertex. The
cycle therefore lives entirely in `Sum.inl`-lifted edges and
projects to a directed cycle in `G` of the same length,
contradicting `G.IsAcyclic`. See the per-theorem comment block above
for the walk-lifting plan; the precondition `hW : W ⊆ G.J ∪ G.V`
appears for *typing* reasons only (it is the well-typedness
condition of `extendingCDMGWithInterventionNodes`), not as a
mathematical hypothesis of acyclicity preservation.

`G` and `W` are implicit because they are unifiable from `hW` and
from the conclusion; `hW` is explicit because
`extendingCDMGWithInterventionNodes` requires it explicitly at every
call site; `h : G.IsAcyclic` is the mathematical hypothesis under
transport. Naming follows Mathlib's `<conclusion>_<construction>`
convention, consistent with `isAcyclic_hardInterventionOn`
(claim_3_3 part A) and `isAcyclic_nodeSplittingOn` (claim_3_6 part
B). -/
theorem isAcyclic_extendingCDMGWithInterventionNodes
    {G : CDMG α} {W : Set α} (hW : W ⊆ G.J ∪ G.V) (h : G.IsAcyclic) :
    (G.extendingCDMGWithInterventionNodes W hW).IsAcyclic := by
  -- Mirrors `tex/claim_3_13_proof_AcyclicityUnderIntervention.tex` Part A.
  -- Step 1: a cycle's start vertex `v` is the target of its closing step, so
  -- by `extendingCDMGWithInterventionNodes_target_isInl` it is
  -- `Sum.inl _`. Step 2: lift the cycle to a directed walk in `G` of the
  -- same length via `walkLiftExtendingCDMGWithInterventionNodes`.
  -- Step 3: contradict `G.IsAcyclic` at the projected vertex.
  rintro v _hv ⟨π, h_dir, h_pos⟩
  -- The end vertex `v` of the cycle is the target of the closing step,
  -- hence `Sum.inl _` by `walkEnd_isInl`.
  obtain ⟨v', hv'⟩ := walkEnd_isInl π h_dir h_pos
  subst hv'
  -- Lift the whole cycle to a directed walk in `G` of the same length.
  obtain ⟨ρ, h_ρ_dir, h_ρ_len⟩ :=
    walkLiftExtendingCDMGWithInterventionNodes hW π h_dir v' v' rfl rfl
  have hρ_pos : 1 ≤ ρ.length := by rw [h_ρ_len]; exact h_pos
  -- `v' ∈ G` follows from the first step of `π`: by piece-1 of
  -- `mem_extendingCDMGWithInterventionNodes_E`, the underlying edge
  -- `(v', x) ∈ G.E` puts `v' ∈ G` via `G.E_subset`.
  have hv'_G : v' ∈ G := by
    cases π with
    | nil _ => simp at h_pos
    | @cons _ m _ s _ =>
      cases s with
      | forward he =>
        have he_ext : (Sum.inl v', m) ∈
            (G.extendingCDMGWithInterventionNodes W hW).E := he
        rw [mem_extendingCDMGWithInterventionNodes_E] at he_ext
        rcases he_ext with ⟨v₁, _, hE, h_eq⟩ | ⟨w', h_eq⟩
        · rw [Prod.mk.injEq] at h_eq
          have hv_eq : v' = v₁ := Sum.inl_injective h_eq.1
          subst hv_eq
          exact CDMG.mem_iff.mpr (Set.mem_prod.mp (G.E_subset hE)).1
        · rw [Prod.mk.injEq] at h_eq
          exact nomatch h_eq.1
      | backward _ => simp at h_dir
      | bidir _ => simp at h_dir
  exact h v' hv'_G ⟨ρ, h_ρ_dir, hρ_pos⟩

/-- The *extension order* `extensionOrder r` on the carrier `α ⊕ ↑S`,
induced by a base relation `r : α → α → Prop` and a parameter
set `S : Set α`. This is the LN's "putting all `I_w` nodes first"
recipe from claim_3_13: every `Sum.inr ⟨w, _⟩` precedes every
`Sum.inl v`, and same-side pairs inherit `r` from the underlying
`α`-values. The four cases of the pattern match correspond to the
four `(Sum.inl/inr, Sum.inl/inr)` corner pairs; see the design
block above and the file-level docstring.

`S` is implicit because it is recovered from the relation's domain
type `α ⊕ ↑S` at the call site; in
`isTopologicalOrder_extendingCDMGWithInterventionNodes_extend` we
spell it `extensionOrder (S := W \ G.J) r` to make the choice
explicit. `noncomputable` because we do not assume any decidability
on `r`; downstream uses are all `Prop`-valued so the classical
choice has no observable cost. Used by
`isTopologicalOrder_extendingCDMGWithInterventionNodes_extend`
(this file) and quoted by name in claim_3_14 / claim_3_15
(commutation) and the iSCM / do-calculus chapters that recurse
along a topological order of an extended CDMG. -/
noncomputable def extensionOrder {S : Set α} (r : α → α → Prop) :
    (α ⊕ ↑S) → (α ⊕ ↑S) → Prop
  | Sum.inl v₁, Sum.inl v₂ => r v₁ v₂
  | Sum.inr ⟨w₁, _⟩, Sum.inr ⟨w₂, _⟩ => r w₁ w₂
  | Sum.inr _, Sum.inl _ => True
  | Sum.inl _, Sum.inr _ => False

/-- claim_3_13 part C: if `W ⊆ G.J ∪ G.V` and `r` is a topological
order of `G`, then `extensionOrder (S := W \ G.J) r` is a topological
order of `G.extendingCDMGWithInterventionNodes W hW`. Mirrors the
constructive extension direction of the `\Rem` immediately after
def_3_13 in `lecture-notes/lecture_notes/graphs.tex` (lines 821 --
829), with the LN's "put all the `I_w` nodes first" recipe encoded
as the four-case `extensionOrder` pattern match.

The `W ⊆ G.J ∪ G.V` precondition is structurally required by
`extendingCDMGWithInterventionNodes` itself (def_3_13) -- the fresh
edge `Sum.inr ⟨w, _⟩ → Sum.inl w` needs its target `Sum.inl w` to
live in the extended graph's `V`-set, which is true only when
`w ∈ G.V` for `w ∈ W \ G.J`. This is not a topological-order
condition; it is the def_3_13 condition transported.

See `isTopologicalOrder_extendingCDMGWithInterventionNodes_restrict`
below for the inverse-direction companion (a topological order of
the extended graph restricts to one of `G`),
`isAcyclic_extendingCDMGWithInterventionNodes` above for the
acyclicity half, and the file-level docstring for the rationale
behind splitting the LN's single `\Rem` into three theorems plus
one named construction. -/
theorem isTopologicalOrder_extendingCDMGWithInterventionNodes_extend
    {G : CDMG α} {W : Set α} (hW : W ⊆ G.J ∪ G.V)
    {r : α → α → Prop} (hr : G.IsTopologicalOrder r) :
    (G.extendingCDMGWithInterventionNodes W hW).IsTopologicalOrder
      (extensionOrder (S := W \ G.J) r) := by
  -- Mirrors `tex/claim_3_13_proof_AcyclicityUnderIntervention.tex` Part C.
  -- The four `IsTopologicalOrder` fields discharge by case-splitting on the
  -- `Sum.inl/inr` carrier; same-side cases reduce to `hr`-fields on
  -- underlying `α`-values, cross cases land in the `True`/`False` slots
  -- of `extensionOrder`. `parent_lt` for piece-2 fresh edges lands
  -- directly in the cross-case `True` slot, no `hr` needed.
  -- Helper: `Sum.inl v ∈ G.ext W hW ↔ v ∈ G`.
  have mem_inl : ∀ {v : α},
      (Sum.inl v : α ⊕ ↑(W \ G.J)) ∈
        G.extendingCDMGWithInterventionNodes W hW ↔ v ∈ G := by
    intro v
    simp only [CDMG.mem_iff, extendingCDMGWithInterventionNodes_J,
      extendingCDMGWithInterventionNodes_V, Set.mem_union, Set.mem_image,
      Set.mem_range]
    constructor
    · rintro ((⟨j, hj, hjv⟩ | ⟨w, hw⟩) | ⟨v', hv', hvv'⟩)
      · cases Sum.inl_injective hjv; exact Or.inl hj
      · exact nomatch hw
      · cases Sum.inl_injective hvv'; exact Or.inr hv'
    · rintro (hJ | hV)
      · exact Or.inl (Or.inl ⟨v, hJ, rfl⟩)
      · exact Or.inr ⟨v, hV, rfl⟩
  -- Helper: every fresh `w' : ↑(W \ G.J)` underlies a node of `G`.
  -- (`w' ∈ W` via `hW : W ⊆ G.J ∪ G.V`; `w' ∉ G.J` forces `w' ∈ G.V`.)
  have inr_mem_G : ∀ w' : ↑(W \ G.J), (w' : α) ∈ G := by
    intro w'
    have hwW : (w' : α) ∈ W := w'.property.1
    have hwJ : (w' : α) ∉ G.J := w'.property.2
    rcases hW hwW with hJ | hV
    · exact absurd hJ hwJ
    · exact Or.inr hV
  refine ⟨?_, ?_, ?_, ?_⟩
  · -- irrefl: case-split on the carrier; both branches collapse
    -- `extensionOrder r v v` to `r v' v'` for `v' ∈ G`.
    rintro (v | ⟨w, hw⟩) hv
    · exact hr.irrefl v (mem_inl.mp hv)
    · exact hr.irrefl (w : α) (inr_mem_G ⟨w, hw⟩)
  · -- trans: case-split on all three carrier shapes (8 cases). The cross
    -- cases `O → F` are `False`, so any case using such a step as a premise
    -- is vacuous. The remaining cases collapse to `hr.trans` on the
    -- underlying `α`-values; the `F → O` cross slot is `True` and absorbs.
    rintro (a | ⟨a, ha⟩) h_a (b | ⟨b, hb⟩) h_b (c | ⟨c, hc⟩) h_c
    · -- (inl a, inl b, inl c)
      intro h_ab h_bc
      exact hr.trans a (mem_inl.mp h_a) b (mem_inl.mp h_b) c (mem_inl.mp h_c)
        h_ab h_bc
    · -- (inl a, inl b, inr c): h_bc : extensionOrder r (inl b) (inr c) = False
      intro _h_ab h_bc
      exact h_bc.elim
    · -- (inl a, inr b, inl c): h_ab : extensionOrder r (inl a) (inr b) = False
      intro h_ab _
      exact h_ab.elim
    · -- (inl a, inr b, inr c): h_ab : extensionOrder r (inl a) (inr b) = False
      intro h_ab _
      exact h_ab.elim
    · -- (inr a, inl b, inl c): h_ab : extensionOrder r (inr a) (inl b) = True;
      -- conclusion: extensionOrder r (inr a) (inl c) = True.
      intro _ _
      trivial
    · -- (inr a, inl b, inr c): h_bc : extensionOrder r (inl b) (inr c) = False
      intro _h_ab h_bc
      exact h_bc.elim
    · -- (inr a, inr b, inl c): conclusion is the cross `True` slot.
      intro _ _
      trivial
    · -- (inr a, inr b, inr c): all three fresh; reduces to `hr.trans` on `(α)`.
      intro h_ab h_bc
      exact hr.trans (a : α) (inr_mem_G ⟨a, ha⟩) b (inr_mem_G ⟨b, hb⟩)
        c (inr_mem_G ⟨c, hc⟩) h_ab h_bc
  · -- trichotomous: case-split on both endpoints (4 cases). Each same-side
    -- case reduces to `hr.trichotomous` on the underlying `α`-values; cross
    -- cases land in the `True` slot.
    rintro (a | ⟨a, ha⟩) h_a (b | ⟨b, hb⟩) h_b
    · -- (inl a, inl b)
      rcases hr.trichotomous a (mem_inl.mp h_a) b (mem_inl.mp h_b) with
        h | rfl | h
      · exact Or.inl h
      · exact Or.inr (Or.inl rfl)
      · exact Or.inr (Or.inr h)
    · -- (inl a, inr b): the cross slot `(inl, inr)` is False, so the only
      -- live disjuncts are middle `=` (impossible by constructor) and the
      -- `(inr, inl)` `True` slot.
      exact Or.inr (Or.inr trivial)
    · -- (inr a, inl b): the cross slot `(inr, inl)` is True.
      exact Or.inl trivial
    · -- (inr a, inr b)
      rcases hr.trichotomous (a : α) (inr_mem_G ⟨a, ha⟩) (b : α)
        (inr_mem_G ⟨b, hb⟩) with h | h_eq | h
      · exact Or.inl h
      · refine Or.inr (Or.inl ?_)
        exact congrArg Sum.inr (Subtype.ext h_eq)
      · exact Or.inr (Or.inr h)
  · -- parent_lt: case-split on the two pieces of
    -- `mem_extendingCDMGWithInterventionNodes_E`.
    intro v w h_pa
    obtain ⟨_, h_vw_E⟩ := h_pa
    change (v, w) ∈ (G.extendingCDMGWithInterventionNodes W hW).E at h_vw_E
    rw [mem_extendingCDMGWithInterventionNodes_E] at h_vw_E
    rcases h_vw_E with ⟨v₁, v₂, hE, h_eq⟩ | ⟨w', h_eq⟩
    · -- Piece 1: (v, w) = (Sum.inl v₁, Sum.inl v₂) with (v₁, v₂) ∈ G.E.
      -- `extensionOrder` fires the `(inl, inl)` case, reducing to `hr.parent_lt`.
      rw [Prod.mk.injEq] at h_eq
      obtain ⟨hv_eq, hw_eq⟩ := h_eq
      subst hv_eq
      subst hw_eq
      have hv₁_G : v₁ ∈ G :=
        CDMG.mem_iff.mpr (Set.mem_prod.mp (G.E_subset hE)).1
      exact hr.parent_lt ⟨hv₁_G, hE⟩
    · -- Piece 2: (v, w) = (Sum.inr w', Sum.inl w'.val). The `(inr, inl)`
      -- cross case of `extensionOrder` fires with `True` -- no `hr` needed.
      rw [Prod.mk.injEq] at h_eq
      obtain ⟨hv_eq, hw_eq⟩ := h_eq
      subst hv_eq
      subst hw_eq
      trivial

/-- claim_3_13 part B: if `W ⊆ G.J ∪ G.V` and `r` is a topological
order of `G.extendingCDMGWithInterventionNodes W hW`, then the
restriction `fun v w => r (Sum.inl v) (Sum.inl w)` is a topological
order of `G`. Mirrors the restriction direction of the `\Rem`
immediately after def_3_13 in
`lecture-notes/lecture_notes/graphs.tex` (lines 821 -- 829, first
sentence second clause: "a topological order for `G_{\doit(I_W)}` is
also one for `G`").

The `W ⊆ G.J ∪ G.V` precondition is structurally required by
`extendingCDMGWithInterventionNodes` itself (def_3_13), not by the
restriction direction per se; see
`isTopologicalOrder_extendingCDMGWithInterventionNodes_extend` for
the matching extension direction and the file-level docstring for
the rationale behind splitting the LN's single `\Rem` into three
theorems plus one named construction.

Inverse-direction companion to the extension theorem: field-by-field
transport using the fact that `Sum.inl` embedding takes `G`'s nodes
into the extended graph's nodes
(`extendingCDMGWithInterventionNodes_J` /
`extendingCDMGWithInterventionNodes_V` make this precise) and
`Sum.inl`-images of `G.E` are edges of the extended graph
(`mem_extendingCDMGWithInterventionNodes_E` first disjunct). -/
theorem isTopologicalOrder_extendingCDMGWithInterventionNodes_restrict
    {G : CDMG α} {W : Set α} (hW : W ⊆ G.J ∪ G.V)
    {r : (α ⊕ ↑(W \ G.J)) → (α ⊕ ↑(W \ G.J)) → Prop}
    (hr : (G.extendingCDMGWithInterventionNodes W hW).IsTopologicalOrder r) :
    G.IsTopologicalOrder (fun v w => r (Sum.inl v) (Sum.inl w)) := by
  -- Mirrors `tex/claim_3_13_proof_AcyclicityUnderIntervention.tex` Part B.
  -- Inverse-direction companion to Part C: field-by-field transport
  -- applying `hr.{irrefl, trans, trichotomous}` at `Sum.inl`-lifted nodes
  -- (membership transported via `extendingCDMGWithInterventionNodes_J`
  -- / `_V`); `parent_lt` lifts `(v, w) ∈ G.E` to `(Sum.inl v, Sum.inl w)
  -- ∈ G_ext.E` via the first disjunct of
  -- `mem_extendingCDMGWithInterventionNodes_E`.
  -- Helper: `Sum.inl v ∈ G.ext W hW` whenever `v ∈ G`.
  have mem_inl : ∀ {v : α}, v ∈ G →
      (Sum.inl v : α ⊕ ↑(W \ G.J)) ∈
        G.extendingCDMGWithInterventionNodes W hW := by
    intro v hv
    simp only [CDMG.mem_iff, extendingCDMGWithInterventionNodes_J,
      extendingCDMGWithInterventionNodes_V, Set.mem_union, Set.mem_image,
      Set.mem_range]
    rcases hv with hJ | hV
    · exact Or.inl (Or.inl ⟨v, hJ, rfl⟩)
    · exact Or.inr ⟨v, hV, rfl⟩
  refine ⟨?_, ?_, ?_, ?_⟩
  · -- irrefl: transport `hr.irrefl` at `Sum.inl v`.
    intro v hv
    exact hr.irrefl (Sum.inl v) (mem_inl hv)
  · -- trans: transport via `hr.trans` at the three `Sum.inl`-lifted vertices.
    intro v hv w hw x hx
    exact hr.trans (Sum.inl v) (mem_inl hv) (Sum.inl w) (mem_inl hw)
      (Sum.inl x) (mem_inl hx)
  · -- trichotomous: transport via `hr.trichotomous`; the middle disjunct
    -- `Sum.inl v = Sum.inl w` collapses to `v = w` by `Sum.inl_injective`.
    intro v hv w hw
    rcases hr.trichotomous (Sum.inl v) (mem_inl hv) (Sum.inl w) (mem_inl hw)
      with h | h_eq | h
    · exact Or.inl h
    · exact Or.inr (Or.inl (Sum.inl_injective h_eq))
    · exact Or.inr (Or.inr h)
  · -- parent_lt: lift `(v, w) ∈ G.E` to a `Sum.inl × Sum.inl`-lifted edge
    -- of the extended graph via piece 1 of
    -- `mem_extendingCDMGWithInterventionNodes_E`; then `hr.parent_lt`.
    intro v w h_pa
    obtain ⟨hv_G, h_vw_E⟩ := h_pa
    have h_lifted : (Sum.inl v, Sum.inl w) ∈
        (G.extendingCDMGWithInterventionNodes W hW).E := by
      rw [mem_extendingCDMGWithInterventionNodes_E]
      exact Or.inl ⟨v, w, h_vw_E, rfl⟩
    exact hr.parent_lt ⟨mem_inl hv_G, h_lifted⟩

lean_explanation:
The LN packs a three-statement remark into a single `\Rem` block: acyclicity preservation, plus the restriction and extension directions of "a topological order of one graph is a topological order of the other". The Lean file splits this into three theorems plus one named construction, all in the `Causality.CDMG` namespace.

Across the file, `α : Type*` is the ambient vertex type — the LN's silent assumption that $G$ lives over some unspecified carrier. `G : CDMG α` is the LN's $G = (J, V, E, L)$, with structure projections `G.J`, `G.V`, `G.E`, `G.L`. The LN's intervention-target set $W$ is `W : Set α`, and the precondition $W \subseteq J \cup V$ is `hW : W ⊆ G.J ∪ G.V`, a `def_3_13` well-typedness condition that pins each fresh edge's target into the extended graph's `V`-set. The carrier of the extended graph is the tagged disjoint union `α ⊕ ↑(W \ G.J)`: `Sum.inl v` corresponds to an original node, while `Sum.inr ⟨w, _⟩` corresponds to the fresh $I_w$ intervention node, indexed by the subtype `↑(W \ G.J)` of $W$-vertices not already in $G$'s inputs.

A handful of Mathlib idioms drive the encoding. `Sum.inl` and `Sum.inr` are the canonical injections into the disjoint union, and `Sum.inl_injective` is invoked to invert. The subtype `↑(W \ G.J) = {x : α // x ∈ W \ G.J}` carries each fresh index together with a membership proof, with field accessors `w'.property.1 : (w' : α) ∈ W` and `w'.property.2 : (w' : α) ∉ G.J`. `IsAcyclic` and `IsTopologicalOrder` are structures from `Section3_1`; the latter bundles four fields `irrefl`, `trans`, `trichotomous`, and `parent_lt`. The acyclicity argument inspects the inductive structure of `Walk` — its `nil`/`cons` shape, the three step constructors `Walk.forward`/`Walk.backward`/`Walk.bidir`, and the `Walk.IsDirected` predicate. The `@[simp]` characterisation `mem_extendingCDMGWithInterventionNodes_E` makes the edge case-split tractable: every edge is either a piece-1 `Sum.inl × Sum.inl`-lift of some $(v_1, v_2) \in G.E$ or a piece-2 fresh edge from `Sum.inr ⟨w, _⟩` to `Sum.inl w`.

The acyclicity theorem takes `hW` and `h : G.IsAcyclic` and concludes that the extended graph is acyclic; three private helpers (excluded from the anchors) lift the directed-cycle argument by showing every edge of the extended graph has target `Sum.inl _`, every directed walk's intermediate vertices are `Sum.inl _`, and a directed walk from `Sum.inl v` to `Sum.inl w` lifts to a walk in $G$ of the same length. The named def `extensionOrder {S : Set α} (r : α → α → Prop) : (α ⊕ ↑S) → (α ⊕ ↑S) → Prop` is the LN's "put all $I_w$ first" recipe, encoded as a four-case pattern match on the `Sum` carrier: same-side pairs inherit `r`, fresh-then-original is `True`, original-then-fresh is `False`. The extension theorem takes `hr : G.IsTopologicalOrder r` and concludes `(G.extendingCDMGWithInterventionNodes W hW).IsTopologicalOrder (extensionOrder (S := W \ G.J) r)`; the restriction theorem inverts via `fun v w => r (Sum.inl v) (Sum.inl w)` along the `Sum.inl` embedding.
design_choices:
**Splitting one `\Rem` into three theorems plus a named construction.** The LN bundles three distinct statements under one remark — acyclicity preservation, restriction of a topological order along the `Sum.inl` embedding, and extension via the "all $I_w$ first" recipe. Different downstream consumers need different halves: `claim_3_14` and `claim_3_15` (commutation with disjoint hard interventions and node-splitting) need acyclicity alone to discharge CADMG side conditions, while the iSCM and do-calculus chapters need the extension order named so they can recurse along it. The split is also *structurally forced* by the carrier change: `extendingCDMGWithInterventionNodes` shifts the vertex type from `α` to `α ⊕ ↑(W \ G.J)`, so a topological order of $G$ and one of the extended graph live on different types and must be transported explicitly. The closest analogue, `claim_3_3` (hard intervention), keeps the carrier fixed and so collapses the two topological-order directions into a single relation; here the directions are genuinely two transports between distinct relation spaces, mirroring the parallel splitting in `claim_3_6` (node splitting).

**`extensionOrder` as a standalone named construction.** The LN's recipe is hedged with "e.g." — one specific extension, not the canonical one. Naming the relation lets downstream rows quote it by name rather than re-deriving the four-case match each time; mirrors `splitOrder` from `claim_3_6`. The parameter is named `S : Set α` rather than hard-coding `W \ G.J`, so the relation composes with arbitrary subtypes; at the use site we spell `extensionOrder (S := W \ G.J) r` explicitly. The construction inspects no structure of $G$, so it takes no `G : CDMG α` argument. It is `noncomputable` because `r` need not be decidable; downstream uses are `Prop`-valued.

**Cross cases as `True` / `False`.** The recipe is asymmetric: every fresh $I_w$ precedes every original node, never the other way. Encoding `(Sum.inr, Sum.inl) := True` and `(Sum.inl, Sum.inr) := False` makes the asymmetry directly visible and lets `trichotomous` and `trans` transport trivially on cross pairs. For `parent_lt`, the cross slot is exactly where the fresh edge from `Sum.inr ⟨w, _⟩` to `Sum.inl w` lands, so `True` discharges the obligation with no appeal to `hr`. The same-side `(Sum.inr, Sum.inr)` case inherits `r` from the underlying `α`-values; the choice is free (no edges between fresh nodes), but inheriting `r` keeps the relation total, transitive, and irreflexive without a side relation on `↑S`.

**Restriction as `fun v w => r (Sum.inl v) (Sum.inl w)`.** The `Sum.inl` embedding is the canonical lift of $G$'s carrier into the extended graph's carrier, so restricting `r` along it is the natural inverse of the `extensionOrder` recipe. This direction is a *Lean-only artefact* in the sense that the LN's "a topological order for $G_{\doit(I_W)}$ is also one for $G$" reads as a trivial syntactic remark when the carrier doesn't change (cf. `claim_3_3`). Because the carrier *does* change here, the sentence becomes substantive content: a literal `Sum.inl`-restriction must be performed to land in the right relation space, and naming it explicitly saves downstream consumers from re-deriving the transport.

**No `[Finite α]` hypothesis; `hW` is typing-only.** Walk-lifting for acyclicity and the four `IsTopologicalOrder` fields all transport via the `@[simp]` membership lemmas of `ExtendingCDMGsWithInterventionNodes.lean`, none of which need finiteness. The precondition `hW : W ⊆ G.J ∪ G.V` is structurally required by `extendingCDMGWithInterventionNodes` itself — it pins each fresh edge's target into the extended graph's `V`-set — but contributes nothing mathematically to acyclicity preservation, mirroring `isAcyclic_nodeSplittingOn` (`claim_3_6` part B).
